I want to talk about the types of unique mechanisms. So there are really, there are really three main types. There are really four types of unique mechanisms, but there are three main types. These are kind of the, and I don't want you to get caught up so much in being like, you know, like black and white. Is this this one? Is this this one? I want you to be able to just put these into, into different categories when you see mechanisms. And you're going to forever see mechanisms by the time we are done. So the first type of mechanism is the existing mechanism. And we're going to break these down. First type is an existing mechanism. And really the simplest way to think about this is this is when there is something just immediately, immediately recognizable as different, as unique. Then there is what's called the unspoken mechanism. This goes back to what somebody asked earlier about the Schlitz-Bier story, which I'm going to share with you. There is what's called the transubstantiated mechanism, which is the most common type of mechanism. And then there is the metaphorical mechanism. And the metaphorical mechanism is really is the most rare, I should say it's the, it's the least common in that, in that there's a special instance when it's used, which I'm going to explain to you. So let's go through this. So some examples of an existing mechanism. And number one is the E5 method. Right? Like there is a very different approach to creating, to setting up campaign, like from beginning to end, there is just a very different approach behind the E5 method. Like it is built in, it's immediately apparent that in the E5 method, there is a different approach to creating campaigns. And similar to this, and these are all the examples that I gave my friend, Ryan Levec, who many of you may know, Ryan Levec is the, is the author of Ask and what is the other one? And choose, he is the creator of the Ask method. Ironically, these both have the first three examples have the word method in there, which I wouldn't read anything into that. But this is a great example because right the, and I want you to check this out. So I want you to notice this. So the Ask method is, and listen to me, please, I know that we've been going for a little bit over two hours already. And some of you are probably hungry, but hang in there. Listen, please. So Ryan has a unique approach to, to creating quizzes, polls, and assessments. He's got a completely different process really, in terms of how he surveys, what questions he asks, how he looks at those questions, how he uses those questions, how he puts together the surveys, the number of questions that he asks, the order in which he asks those questions, the type of questions that he asks, he has a whole process. Now, if you, if you look at this, and right, Ryan is a good friend of mine, I've spoken for him at years ago, at a couple of his masterminds for his, for his folks. And, and what I said to his folks even, I said, like, look, right, Ryan, and this is what I want you to get, Ryan doesn't talk about how to grow a business or do better list segmentation with quizzes and polls and assessments. He doesn't talk about how to do better list segmentation and get a responsive list with quizzes, polls, and, you know, and surveys. Because if that's what he did, quizzes, polls, and surveys are a commoditized mechanism. They are available anywhere. You could go to Google and do a search and find quizzes in marketing, surveys in marketing, polls in marketing, assessments in marketing. You could go and find YouTube videos on it, blog posts on it, other people talking about quizzes, polls, surveys. No, Ryan doesn't teach that. Ryan teaches his process of using polls, quizzes, and surveys. His process, which is called the ask method, the ask method, which is different from the common approach to, to using quizzes and polls and, and, and, and surveys. And Ryan can explain as he does, and he does it really well, he can explain what makes it different and what makes it more effective than the typical approach to surveys, polls and whatnot. So Ryan, when Ryan talks about the, the, the outcome, the result, he's talking about the result that you can experience from the ask method. When he is sharing success stories, he's sharing success stories, not of people that used quizzes and polls and surveys, but people that used the ask method. When Ryan is demonstrating results, he's not demonstrating results from quizzes, polls, and surveys. He's demonstrating results of the ask method. The ask method is his unique mechanism. His ask method masterclass, I believe, which it's called, is the product. So when Ryan talks about the ask method masterclass, right, and what you get with it and what it'll do for you, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, that's the product. But when Ryan is talking about the ask method, that is his mechanism. And right, and he differentiates his mechanism, he contrasts his mechanism against the typical way of doing polls surveys, right. And so nobody, by the time you're done with one of Ryan's campaigns, right, and I've spoken to him about this, on his campaigns and doing this at length, right, people are not bought into simple quizzes, polls, and surveys, they're bought into the ask method for using polls, quizzes, and surveys, just like with Ted, right, with Ted, when we talked about, right, like Ted is not selling people on LinkedIn marketing, he's selling people on his method of using LinkedIn to generate more sales, just like Ryan is leading people to see the value of his method of using and deploying surveys and whatnot. And like Terry said, he does it very well. He's, he understands this principle of unique mechanism and educating on the value of the unique mechanism, getting buy-in on the unique mechanism, and then offering the way for them to get the unique mechanism, right. So he convinces you of the value of the ask method, shows you what it can do, shows you who's used it and what results it's produced, proves why his method of deploying surveys, polls, and quizzes, the ask method is superior to the typical way. And then once you see, man, the ask method, the ask method sounds amazing, not polls, quizzes, surveys, the ask method sounds amazing. And then he offers you the opportunity to get into his masterclass, which is the fulfillment of the ask method. Same thing with, so I don't know how many of you, if any of you know Kurt Molley, Kurt Molley is like a Facebook dude, a Facebook wizard. And so a couple of years ago, I had some of the top media buyers in the direct response world. I had 40, 45 of them, something like that in Baltimore. I think actually JMC, who I believe is on here, was in attendance, I believe, like, you know, folks from, you know, Purple, Mattress, folk, the media buyer from Tai Lopez, Agora, ClickFunnels, Onyx and Gal, Keith Kranz, like it was, it was stacked. And in the end, they all voted for who they felt, which media buyer they felt gave the best value over the over the two days. And I awarded that person a belt, a heavyweight championship belt that I had specifically created. That's where Kurt got the belt method, right? That after he, I gave him, I awarded him that belt. He decided to kind of formalize his, his process. So when Kurt talks about using, you know, getting traffic from Facebook and what, he is not talking about Facebook advertising. He's not educating on Facebook advertising. He's not building the value around Facebook advertising. He's not getting you bought into Facebook advertising. He's getting you bought into the belt method of using Facebook advertising, his process, his methodology, his approach that he happens to call the belt method. And then I believe he also has the belt method masterclass. And so, so there you go, Glenn, I dig it. And so I love the ask method so much. I became an ask method certified pro. Great. I mean, Ryan is a sharp cookie. I can share with you one of the very first webinars that I believe he ever did with me. We both, I have a lot less gray. I don't think I had a beard yet. And he was like this little, it was, it was awesome. And he's, he's an amazing dude. And yeah, and Kurt, you know, like my point, you see the similarity here, right? Like that Kurt not getting buy in on Facebook advertising, not getting, not teaching you Facebook advertising, he's getting buy in on the belt method. And what is the belt method? It's just his method for, for doing or using Facebook advertising. Right. And so when you think about, right, I could do, I could learn Facebook advertising, like, or I could learn the belt method, the belt method is proprietary, it's only available through Kurt. So who was it earlier? I think it was, was it Ryan, Ryan asked earlier about all roads lead to you. Well, let's say you hear a presentation about the belt method, let's say hypothetically, even let's just make it real simple and say that, that you heard two, three, four people on a live stream talking about how great the belt method is, right? And that their experience with it and what it's done for their business and how unbelievable the belt method is and how they struggled with Facebook before Facebook advertising. And now they're experiencing great results and they just credit the belt method for, for all that. Well, now, right, the only place to get it is from Kurt, you cannot go and learn the belt method anywhere else that is an all roads lead to Kurt position, because it is a proprietary solution. Now imagine that all those people were talking about Facebook advertising and how they used Facebook advertising and how Facebook advertising changed their business. And now, right, Facebook advertising was the game changer and how they love it and it's great and all been all different. Now they just opened up Pandora's box because now you can go and you can research, you can start to look around, you could start to maybe do some research on your own. Let me see if I could learn some stuff from tutorials from YouTube. Let me read a book, let me go to Amazon and get Facebook advertising for dummies or whatever, right? And so see how that is a, you know, that is, you know, roads lead everywhere. Whereas when you have a unique mechanism and you focus on getting buy in on the unique mechanism, you create an all roads lead to you path, all roads lead to you path. And so belt method. Last one, which is similar is my buddy Jeff Walker. And so some of you may know Jeff. And so Jeff has been around a long time. Jeff and I have known each other for gosh, I don't even know 12, 13 years, something like that now at this point, 15 years maybe. And so Jeff has, Jeff has product launch formula. Now think about this for one second. Okay. And hopefully this is kind of starting to make sense for you that, right? Like so Jeff is not though teaching how to launch a product, right? If you want to know, how do I launch a product? How do I market a new product? Go to Google and you'll find millions of listings. Go to YouTube. You'll find tons and tons of videos on how to launch a product. No, what Jeff is teaching you is his product launch formula, his process, his method, his system for launching a product, right? And so when Jeff is educating, he's not educating on the value of launching a product. He's educating on the value of launching a product with product launch formula, right? When he's showing results, he's not showing results of what people can achieve when they launch a product properly. He's showing what people, what can be achieved when people launch their product with product launch formula with PLF, right? And so he is not, he's not presenting the generic. If you want to know how to launch a product, go and you could do some research and you can find a ton of stuff. You can find books, you can find YouTube videos, you can find blog posts, but if you want to know PLF, if you want to know product launch formula, right, Jeff or one of his licensees are the only source because he is the only purveyor of PLF, just like Kurt is the only purveyor of the belt method, just like Ryan is the only purveyor of the ask method, just like I, we are the only source of the E5 method. And so, right? That's Ryan, when it's all roads lead to you because once you want the mechanism, you can only go to one place to get it. My aim for each and every one of you is to have you be that one place, right? That one, that one source. And so in here, what you're talking about is a fully fleshed out methodology. It's an existing mechanism because there is a different process in each of these cases to what the common, there is an actual different process, different set of steps, different system, different approach. Some deep, the belt method is a very deep method. Even Ryan within the ask method has, you know, he's got the deep dive survey, which right, he's got, you know, like if you look at all of these, in all of these instances, and we're going to talk more about this later, these are unique mechanisms that are, they're frameworks, they're big frameworks that consist of a lot of different steps. And even within those different steps, there are different unique mechanisms. So the E5 method, the ask method, the belt method, the product launch formula are all in and of themselves unique mechanisms. But then you take, let's say, the ask method, you can, Glenn can attest to this. And he's, Ryan's got his deep dive survey thing, which is his approach to surveying your audience to get the information that you need, right? And there's a certain way, there's a certain set of questions, and there's a certain, you know, there's a certain structure and process to it that and of itself, right, is, is a, is a, you know, and, and so each of these, right, these are existing mechanisms. And still the cornerstone of the research method for us. That's awesome. I love that. Right. And that you think of, you think of deep dive survey, who's that linked to? It's linked to one person. It's linked to Ryan, right? Deep dive survey, link to Ryan, think of seed launch, link to Jeff Walker, right? You think of even ironically as, as meta as this sounds, you think of the unique mechanism, Todd, right? E5 method. Segmentation survey, traffic measurement, I love it. It's awesome. See how that, see how both, um, right, like see how, um, and it's Phillip, right? See, and sin offer, JMC, absolutely. Glenn, right? You see how there's a process there, right? Ryan, you know, it's not, it wasn't like, let me teach you how to do surveys better, right? I'm going to teach you how to do surveys better. I'm going to teach you surveys or I'm a wizard when it comes to surveys. And so I'm going to teach you the right way to do surveys. Everybody else was, he had a unique method, a unique process, a unique framework, a unique system, a unique set of steps that, that, right, that he named. That's really all it is that I want you to understand. It's the identifying of it, the naming it, and then the, the deploying it, which you're going to learn. And so here, so the unspoken mechanism, uh, the unspoken mechanism, I forget who mentioned this earlier about the Schlitz beer, um, story, but there was this, you know, there's this story whether legend or not that, um, Schlitz beer was losing market share. And so Schlitz beer brought in Claude Hopkins, one of the Godfathers of direct response of scientific marketing, scientific advertising, and, um, and for, for help. And so Claude Hopkins said, let me take a tour of the, you know, one of your manufacturing plants. And so while he was being taken on the tour of the manufacturing plant, which by the way, as a side note, there's a lesson right there in and of itself, right, that Claude Hopkins didn't just sit down and assume that he understood what was going on. He wanted to see how they do what they do, why they do what they do, where they do what they do, when they do what they do, like he dug in, right. He did, he examined, so to speak, right. He didn't just start writing copies and like take me on a tour of the, of the manufacturing, um, facility. And so while he was being taken on tour, he saw something going on in the, in the corner or whatever, what's going on over there. And, um, and the guy taking him on the tour said, oh, that's where we sterilize all the bottles, you know, and we sterilize all the bottles before we, before we put the beer in there. And Claude Hopkins said, well, why aren't you telling anybody that? And the guy said to him, because every beer manufacturer does that, every beer manufacturer has a sterilization process. And Claude Hopkins said, but nobody is telling the market. And so Claude created campaigns around their process for purity, um, of the, the beer, like their, their sterilization process. Um, and you could see here, he knows purity in filtered air, right. Things that like he, he recognized that just because others are doing, and this is, there's an important point that you hear that I want to give you, just because others are doing it, if others are not, if meaning competitors are doing it, if others are not talking about it to the marketplace, you can use it as a unique mechanism. So what I want you to understand is that unique mechanism, the word unique applies to prospects, meaning that is it unique to prospects? Is it unique in their eyes? Will they view it as unique? Not, is it unique amongst competitors? Because even if your competitors are doing it, but if they're not talking about it and you talk about it, it can be a unique mechanism for you, right? And so it could be a unique mechanism for you. This is a perfect example. So Claude Hopkins, after this campaign, he reacquired a tremendous amount of market share way back for Schlitz beer, just by identifying, seeing that they were doing something that helped with the purity, if you will, of the beer, that even though other competitors were doing it, they weren't talking about it. And so he talked about it. And now this became the point of differentiation. And so the unspoken mechanism, the idea here is that, is that this goes back to the questions that were asked about, about, you know, like, well, what if I'm doing the very same thing as everybody else? And let's say you're doing the identical, like to the T identical thing that everybody else is doing. Well, what part of that process, which is responsible for producing results, are competitors not talking about in their marketing that you could talk about. So just because you're doing the same exact thing to the T, if that was the case, which I would have to, again, I would question you on that, just by talking about it, it gives you the ability to have a unique mechanism. So the unspoken mechanism is a part, an aspect of your process, your system, your approach, your framework, that is not unique amongst competitors, but competitors aren't talking about it. Therefore, you can use it as a, as a mechanism. Then we've got the transubstantiated mechanism. And this term transubstantiated, transubstantiation, it was presented to me, I first learned this many, many years ago from a gentleman by the name of Bill Bonner. Bill is the founder of Agora, the Agora companies, which is like a $1.5 billion direct response company today. They're a client of mine. I've done consulting for, I think, a half a dozen different entities for them, both domestically and abroad. And the idea of transubstantiation comes from Roman Catholicism, and it's the turning the wine into the blood of Christ. And really, it's turning the ordinary into the extraordinary. It's taking the ordinary and turning it into the extraordinary. And this is the most common kind of way of that mechanisms are created. It doesn't mean that it's the best, right? The ideally, you've got an existing mechanism, which I'm going to walk you through the process of, of, of, of potentially identifying that for you. But this is the most common. And so let's take a tripwire. How many of you know what a tripwire is? If you know what a tripwire is, just put Y in the, in the chat. Let's put a Y. Here's how many of you know what a tripwire is? Yeah, everybody. Yep. No. Okay. Did I see a no? Okay. Interesting. Okay. Very cool. So, so tripwire, just since we had one no, is an ultra low priced offer. It's an ultra low barrier of entry offer, low ticket offer designed to make it, you know, brain dead simple for somebody to say yes, right? To reduce barrier of entry by reducing financial risk by making it an easy, an easy yes. Close. You're close, Kevin. It wasn't Russell. And Russell's a dear friend of mine as well, but it wasn't Russell who came up with it. Who knows who came up with who first coin tripwire? Yes, exactly. You got it. Yep. There it is. Ryan digital marketer. All good that you're new. Totally cool. Harry and Ryan. Exactly. Right. And so, Harry and Ryan. Now, let me ask you this. So, right, they were the guys that first coined tripwire offer, ultra low priced offer, right? Low ticket offer, low barrier of entry offer. But, and this was, I don't even know, maybe I remember when they introduced it, I'm not sure 15 years ago, 12 years ago, something like that. I don't even remember now at this point, but let's say 12, 13 years, let's say 12 years ago, and I could be wrong on that. But so, right, the question is, right, did a low ticket, low barrier of entry offer exist prior to 12 years ago? In other words, was nobody prior to 12 years ago using an ultra low ticket, an ultra low ticket offer? Of course they were, right? It was all over. It was being used, right? Low ticket offers have been around since the beginning of time, right? Like, in fact, you could make the case, right? And this is, Ryan and Perry are also friends. This is not a knock against them. This is a tip of the hat to them, right? This is an example of a, of a, of a smart move. They saw low ticket offers being used, right? And what they did was they took it, they made it part of their overall system and they transubstantiated it from low ticket, low cost, low barrier of entry offer into the tripwire, which became an industry wide term now used, right? But they, they took the ordinary and bundled it and made it part of something bigger and turned it from the ordinary into the extraordinary by talking about a tripwire. If they would have said an ultra low ticket, low priced offer, right? Well, now you're talking about a commodity. If you're talking about, you need to learn a tripwire offer. Well, who was the only source to learn the tripwire offer when they introduced it to the marketplace? They were. Therefore, if you wanted to learn about a tripwire offer and how to use a tripwire offer, right, you had to go to them, right? To learn a tripwire as part of their, their overall system. And so that was an example. It's a simple example of a, of an ordinary, an ordinary mechanism turned into something extraordinary by bringing it in and making it part of, of something else. I have, which I don't talk about all that often, but I had, I had, I used to use on a really, on a very regular basis. We still do actually what's called a reverse add on offer. Reverse add on offer was basically, is basically an upsell that is priced lower than the initial product. And so let's say you're selling a product for a hundred bucks and you have an upsell that is priced at 40 bucks, right? And so you sell something for a hundred dollars and then you offer them an add on to that purchase for, for $40, right? And, and it crushes it, it kills it, it knocks it out of the park. And that's because just as a side note, right? That's the typical way that commerce happens, right? Like what's taught online is when you, when you, when you're creating an upsell to create something that's three times the price point of what it is that you just offered. If you just sold something for a hundred bucks, offer an upsell for a hundred, excuse me, $300. But the reality is where in the world of commerce do you go and do you buy something? And then are you offered something three times the price, right? You don't go in and buy, you know, buy an iPhone charger and you're offered an iMac, right? Like it doesn't work that way. You go, you know, right? Like you go in and you buy a men's suit for $3,000. You're offered, you know, a $200 shirt, you're offered, you know, $200 shoes, you're offered something that is a fraction of the price. And so, right, I talked about it as a reverse add-on and I talked about how a reverse add-on produced significant conversions, which it does because it taps into what is expected from consumers. And so the, right, that's an example of the ordinary, the, what was the ordinary? The ordinary was, you know, an upsell price lower than the core offer, but positioning it as a reverse add-on offer changes the frame. Invisible funnel. This came from Russell. Invisible funnel was a, basically a soft offer where somebody comes to, you know, like a webinar and then at the end of the webinar, they are pitched on a product. So I forget exactly what it is, but he called the Invisible webinar. He didn't just say, what you want to do is you want to run a webinar where you don't charge anybody and then at the end you sell them on something else. He called it Invisible funnel. And you could see how all of these are just names like given to a method, like a method that was brought together, right, and just given a name, not using the commoditized name. How many of you are familiar with Profit First? So Profit First, cool process, right? If you really think about Profit First, Profit First, which is the method, the framework, the process, the system, there's a book about it, Mike McCallow, Profit First is basically is really simple. It is this method of a business owner taking profit, taking a distribution off of the gross profit, not waiting for the net profit. So meaning that you have revenue and then you have cost of goods sold. You have what, you like if you sell a physical product or if you have traffic, then you have gross profit and then out of the gross profit, below gross profit, you have things like your internet, your team, your software, your tech, and then there is net profit. Net profit is bottom line and the typical entrepreneur takes their profit distribution from the bottom, from net profit. Well, Mike's whole process is based on taking your distribution as an owner from the gross profit before your operating expenses. Pretty simple, pretty straightforward, pretty ordinary, but Profit First turns that ordinary accounting process into a mechanism. And there are just tons of examples of, I won't even bore you with these. These are all just names given to, like the $50 marijuana stock blueprint was a promotion from the guys at Stansbury Research that was about how you can get started as an investor in the cannabis space with $50. And so you're talking about how to invest in cheap cannabis stocks, starting with only $50, but they called it the $50 marijuana stock blueprint. And so it's just taking the ordinary and giving it a name that substantiates it into something extraordinary, if that makes sense. The last one, and then we're going to take a break for for lunch, is the metaphorical mechanism. And so the metaphorical mechanism works like this. This is different. I said that the, this is, even though this is considered the fourth type of mechanism, unique mechanism, this is really a, this is what you would use when you are marketing and selling a pure commodity, like a commoditized when what you are selling is, let's say there is zero difference there, like the, the, what you're ultimately marketing is already known, is already available, is already out there. It is a pure commodity. Like there is, there is no system or strategy or approach. Well, when there's no system strategy approach, when there's nothing unique, when there's nothing different, if you lead with that commodity, and I'm going to, I'm going to break this down for you so that you understand this, you immediately trigger with prospects, what's called mental opt out where they're like, I've heard it, I've seen it. This promotion right here was one of the most successful promotions in the financial publishing industry, maybe a longer than a decade ago for sure, maybe two decades ago. And what this was about, well, you can see the secret currency, how the world's wealthiest families make money even when stocks, bonds and real estate lose value. This entire promotion, this entire campaign was about, there's a secret currency that exists that lies beyond the reach of any government. It's been used, right? It can protect your wealth. It was this whole case that was, that was, that was built to show value in this secret currency, but at no point in time in this promotion was the secret currency ever revealed or what the secret currency was. And then there was a report that was offered in the end that you could get on how to take advantage of this secret currency and bring it into your portfolio and blah, blah, blah. And, but what the secret currency was was gold. And so it was just about investing in gold. That was it. That was it. There was no unique method to investing in gold, no unique process to investing in gold. It was just about investing in gold, but rather than say, right, it, how to invest in gold, which at the time was and still is a commodity, it's available, that type of message, that type of offer, that type of hook is available everywhere. There's nothing unique, nothing different about that. It's just more of the same. So what you see here is a metaphor, if you will, you know, a way to kind of a fancy pants way to talk about the commodity without talking about the commodity. And you typically, when you see a metaphorical mechanism being used, you'll typically see quotations around it. So when there's quotations around it, that is a good indicator that for you that they're using a metaphorical mechanism, that it's not, there is no real like secret currency. They're not going to reveal to you a, like a real secret currency, a new currency or something like that. I mean, even if they were to say the new currency, right, it's not that they're, they're trying to say there really is a new currency. If the quotes show you that that's what they're, that's what they're calling it. So meaning that think of a metaphorical mechanism as a nickname, a way that you refer to it without referring to it as the commodity. So if you were talking about, you know, retargeting, and you were just talking about retargeting, maybe you don't talk about, you don't call it retargeting, you, you have a nickname that you call it, right, the, you know, personal, personal, whatever, advertising thing, and you put quotes around it. So it's when you have a pure commodity, and you, you have a pure commodity, you're going to use your nickname, your metaphorical name or metaphorical positioning for it. Canadian Social Security is one of my favorites. And so, so David, the metaphor, the metaphorical mechanism is just a way of taking a commodity, commoditized solution, a commoditized method, a commoditized process, and talking about it without trying to say that I've got a new thing, like without saying here the new investment method, right, because you can't say that because that would be a lie, because investing in gold is not a new method. Now, if they had a new method of investing in gold, a new process of investing in gold, that's one thing, right, if they had a method, a system, a process, a framework, that's one thing, they could then, they could then name that unique mechanism, that process, that framework, that system. But if they were just talking about gold, they can't say the new, the new, you know, the new investment for you to get into, because that would be a lie, it's not a new investment. So how do you talk about a commodity without lying while telling the truth, right, but not revealing that what you're talking about is pure, like is the pure commodity, you use the metaphorical mechanism. And so you're basically, you know, for lack of a better phrase for you guys, I would say you're hiding it behind the nickname. So you're hiding it behind the nickname, in this case, secret currency, you know, and we'll talk a little bit about towards, you know, when we get into, like actually fleshing this out and identifying your mechanism and naming and all that stuff, you know, like this is, you know, like this comes about through, through examination, through research, through, you know, the gentleman who wrote this is Mike Palmer, one of the greatest financial copywriters ever, right. And the question becomes, well, we're going to talk about gold, but how do we talk about gold without talking without, without naming gold, because if we name gold, they immediately prospects are going to say, heard it, seen it, been there, done that, Erty, right, goes back to prospect awareness, excuse me, the prospect, the promise exposure spectrum. Right. So how do we talk about gold without calling it gold, right. Well, now when they look back, right, in the history of gold, when they dig in, when they do a little bit of, when they did a little bit of research, they were able to find that there was a time when gold was used kind of hush hush way back medieval times, through certain governments, it was always used for bar, it was always used in trade, even regardless of what was going on with the main currency, gold was always being swapped. Well, that's like a secret currency, secret currency. So very, this is not common, right. This, you don't see this nearly as often as you see the other mechanisms, but this is definitely an option. This is the option that you can go with when you are talking about a pure commodity. So we've looked at a variety of levels here, right. So we looked at, you know, you've got the existing mechanism when, you know, I'm going to teach you how to first and foremost, we want to, we want to dig in, we want to look to see, do you have an existing mechanism? We never want to immediately go to this default to, I don't have a, you know, I don't have an existing mechanism, I'm going to show you how to interrogate in many, many, many cases, the overwhelming majority cases you do. But if you don't, right, you could go on, is there an unspoken mechanism? Is there a piece of this that my competitors are doing using that I'm using that is responsible for producing the result, but they're not talking about it that we can talk about it? Is there a, you know, a transubstantiated mechanism that we can use? What is the ordinary, you know, like what are the different pieces of what it is that I'm doing? What are the ordinary ways that they're talked about that they're, that they're, that they're talked about? How can I take one of those or more of those and transubstantiated? Or am I just selling a pure commodity and, and therefore I need a metaphorical mechanism? This is why I say that regardless of what it is that you have, regardless of what it is that you are offering, you can absolutely have a, a, a unique mechanism.
